title: Koshland Park - San Francisco, California
content: # Koshland Park. Tucked at the corner of Page and Buchanan streets in San Francisco's **Hayes Valley** neighborhood, Koshland Park offers a peaceful retreat from city life. This green space came to be in 1973 when the children of **Daniel E. The park's story has a bittersweet beginning. Make a reservation at this park. Like many urban spaces, the park went through a rough patch in the '80s and '90s when neighborhood crime made it a place many avoided. But in true San Francisco spirit, the community refused to give up on their green space. The **Hayes Valley Neighborhood Parks Group** (HVNPG) formed and sparked a renaissance, eventually securing $2 million to renovate Koshland and two other neighborhood parks. One of the park's standout features is the **Community Learning Garden**, established in 2000 in the lower east section. The playground here is a hit with the little ones, featuring bright play structures and a sand pit that keeps kids entertained for hours.

title: Koshland Park Isn’t Just About a Gate - Hayes Valley San Francisco
content: # Koshland Park Isn’t Just About a Gate. A local park, a proposed change, and a process that left neighbors with more questions than answers. On Saturday, neighbors gathered at Koshland Park for a Recreation & Park hosted open house on the upcoming playground renewal project. What was presented as an opportunity to learn more and provide input instead left many attendees with more questions than answers. The format, an informal drop-in session with no agenda or presentation, made it difficult to understand what was being proposed, how decisions would be made, or how community input would be incorporated. As neighbors compared notes during and after the session, a consistent theme emerged: confusion about the process, uncertainty about participation, and a lack of clear information about potential changes, particularly around access to the community garden. For many neighbors, this week’s confusion was not an isolated moment. For a space that has long functioned as part of the broader park experience, that represents a meaningful change.
